subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 14! In this unit you will become a pattern detective and a chance explorer. Patterns are all around you, every single day. When Amina lays out the prayer mats in neat rows, when Yusuf sees the stripes on his football shirt, or when Halima threads red and white beads for Eid, they are all working with patterns. And chance is everywhere too – when you toss a coin to decide who kicks first, or wonder whether it will rain before you walk to the masjid, you are thinking about probability.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra pattern puzzles, invent new chance games, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why do patterns and probability matter so much? Patterns help us predict what comes next, and predicting is one of the most useful things a mind can do. A farmer who spots the pattern of the seasons knows when to plant. A trader who notices the pattern of busy market days knows when to bring more mangoes. Probability helps us make good choices when we are not sure what will happen. Knowing that rain is likely, Khadija carries a shawl to the masjid. Knowing that a bag of only red beans can never give a blue bean, Omar does not waste a wish on it. Mathematics gives us calm, clear ways to think about both certainty and surprise.

As you work, keep some small objects beside you – beads, buttons, stones, bottle caps, or dry beans in two colours. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Make the patterns with your hands, toss a real coin, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every step you take here builds the thinking skills you will use for the rest of your life.
Recognise a repeating pattern and tell it apart from a random muddle.
Find the rule (the part that repeats) in a pattern and name it, such as AB or ABC.
Copy, continue and create repeating patterns with colours, shapes, sounds and movements.
Fill in the missing part of a pattern by using its rule.
Tell the difference between a repeating pattern and a growing pattern.
Use the chance words certain, likely, unlikely and impossible correctly.
Say whether one event is more likely, less likely or equally likely than another.

concepts.title: What Makes Something a Pattern?
concepts.explanation: A pattern is not just any group of things. A pattern is things arranged so that a part repeats in a set order, over and over, in a way you can predict. That word predict is the heart of it: if you can look at what has come so far and know what comes next, you are looking at a pattern. Imagine Aisha threads beads on a string for Eid like this: red, white, red, white, red, white. You can feel the beat of it, can you not? Red, white… red, white… If someone hid the string and asked, ‘What bead comes next?’ you would…
concepts.example: Patterns do not have to be made of colours. They can be shapes (circle, triangle, circle, triangle), sounds (clap, stamp, clap, stamp), movements (jump, spin, jump, spin), or even the days that repeat each week. What…

concepts.title: Finding the Rule and Naming It
concepts.explanation: Every repeating pattern has a rule – the little piece that repeats. Finding this piece is like finding the key to a door. Once you have it, the whole pattern opens up. To find the rule, look from the start and ask, ‘Where does the pattern begin to say the same thing again?’ That first chunk, up to the point where it repeats, is your rule. Look at this pattern: mango, mango, banana, mango, mango, banana. Where does it start again? After ‘mango, mango, banana’ it says ‘mango, mango, banana’ once more. So the rule is…
concepts.example: To make patterns easy to talk about, mathematicians give the parts letters. The first kind of thing is A, the next new thing is B, the next new thing is C, and so on. This lets us name a pattern quickly without caring…

concepts.title: Copying, Continuing and Creating Patterns
concepts.explanation: There are three things a pattern detective can do with a pattern, and they get a little harder each time. You can copy a pattern, you can continue a pattern, and you can create a brand-new pattern of your own. Let us take them one at a time. Copying is the gentlest. Someone shows you red, blue, blue, red, blue, blue and you lay out the very same beads underneath: red, blue, blue, red, blue, blue. To copy well, look at one item at a time and match it. Copying trains your eyes to notice each part in order, which you…
concepts.example: Continuing means carrying a pattern onward. You are given the start – say, green, yellow, green, yellow, green, ___ – and you must add what comes next. To do this, first find the rule (here it is AB: green, yellow),…

concepts.title: Missing Parts and Repeating vs Growing Patterns
concepts.explanation: Sometimes a pattern has a gap in the middle, and your job is to fill it. This is trickier than continuing, because you cannot just look at the end – you must use the rule to work out what belongs in the hole. Take this one: red, blue, ___, red, blue, green, red, blue, green. The rule is ABC (red, blue, green), so the gap must be green. The way to solve a gap is always the same: find the rule from the parts you can see, then place the missing term the rule demands. There is also a second family of patterns you…
concepts.example: How do you tell the two apart? Ask yourself: ‘Does a chunk come again and again in the same order?’ If yes, it is a repeating pattern. Or ask: ‘Does each step get bigger or smaller by the same amount?’ If yes, it is a…

concepts.title: The Language of Chance
concepts.explanation: Now we turn from patterns to probability, which is a big word for a simple idea: how likely something is to happen. Some things are sure, some things can never happen, and many things are somewhere in between. Mathematicians use four special words to describe where an event sits, and you will use them all through this unit. Certain means it will definitely happen – there is no doubt at all. It is certain that the sun will rise tomorrow. It is certain that if a bag holds only red beans, the bean you draw will be…
concepts.example: Between these two live most everyday events. Likely means it will probably happen, though it is not completely sure. In Mogadishu it is likely to be hot at midday. Unlikely means it will probably not happen, though it…

concepts.title: Comparing How Likely Events Are
concepts.explanation: It is useful not only to describe one event, but to compare two events and say which is more likely. For this we use three phrases: more likely, less likely and equally likely. Comparing chances is often easier than it sounds, because you can look at the objects and count. Think of a bag with 8 red beans and 2 white beans. If you close your eyes and draw one bean, are you more likely to get red or white? There are far more red beans, so drawing red is more likely and drawing white is less likely. The colour with…
concepts.example: The same idea works for a spinner – a circle split into coloured parts with an arrow that spins. If the spinner has a big green part and a small yellow part, the arrow is more likely to stop on green, because green…

methods.title: How to find and continue a pattern
methods.example: Work through these with me. Cover the solution, try it yourself first, then check every step.
steps: Read the pattern from the start and find where it repeats.
Name the repeating chunk as letters (AB, ABC, AAB…).
Check the rule fits all along the pattern.
Keep the rule going to add the next terms, or to fill a gap.

methods.title: How to judge how likely an event is
methods.example: Give the letter name for this pattern: circle, square, square, circle, square, square.
steps: Ask: can it never happen? Then it is impossible.
Ask: will it definitely happen? Then it is certain.
Check the how to judge how likely an event is result carefully.

methods.title: Otherwise decide: is it probable (likely) or not probable (unlikely)?
methods.example: Carry this on for three more terms: green, yellow, green, yellow, green, ___, ___, ___.
steps: To compare two events, count the objects – more objects means more likely.
Check the otherwise decide: is it probable (likely) or not probable (unlikely)? result carefully.
Check the otherwise decide: is it probable (likely) or not probable (unlikely)? result carefully.

methods.title: How to run a chance experiment
methods.example: Find the missing term: red, blue, green, red, ___, green, red, blue, green.
steps: Predict what you think will happen.
Do the test many times in the same fair way.
Record each result with a tally mark.
Count and compare your results with your prediction.

methods.title: Certain, likely, unlikely or impossible?
methods.example: Find the missing term: red, blue, green, red, ___, green, red, blue, green.
steps: Solution: The rule is ABC (red, blue, green).
After red should come blue, so the gap is blue.
Check it: red, blue, green, red, blue, green, red, blue, green – the rule holds all the way, so blue is correct.
Worked Example 5: Certain, likely, unlikely or impossible?

methods.title: More likely or less likely?
methods.example: Find the missing term: red, blue, green, red, ___, green, red, blue, green.
steps: Solution: The rule is ABC (red, blue, green).
After red should come blue, so the gap is blue.
Check it: red, blue, green, red, blue, green, red, blue, green – the rule holds all the way, so blue is correct.
Worked Example 5: Certain, likely, unlikely or impossible?

workedExamples.title: Is it a pattern?
workedExamples.prompt: Work through these with me. Cover the solution, try it yourself first, then check every step.
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1: Is it a pattern? Look at this: red, white, red, white, red, white. Is it a pattern? What about: red, white, blue, red, green, white? Solution: The first one is a pattern, because the chunk ‘red, white’ repeats in the same order over and over, so you can predict red comes next. The second one is not a pattern – no chunk repeats in order, so you cannot predict what comes next. It is a random arrangement.

workedExamples.title: Name the rule
workedExamples.prompt: Give the letter name for this pattern: circle, square, square, circle, square, square.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The repeating chunk is ‘circle, square, square’. Circle is the first new thing (A) and square is the second new thing (B), and square repeats, so the pattern is ABB. It fits all the way along: A-B-B, A-B-B.

workedExamples.title: Continue the pattern
workedExamples.prompt: Carry this on for three more terms: green, yellow, green, yellow, green, ___, ___, ___.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The rule is AB (green, yellow). The last shown term is green, so next comes yellow, then green, then yellow. The full carry-on is yellow, green, yellow.

workedExamples.title: Fill the missing part
workedExamples.prompt: Find the missing term: red, blue, green, red, ___, green, red, blue, green.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The rule is ABC (red, blue, green). After red should come blue, so the gap is blue. Check it: red, blue, green, red, blue, green, red, blue, green – the rule holds all the way, so blue is correct. Worked Example 5: Certain, likely, unlikely or impossible? A bag holds only white beans. Choose the right word for each: (a) drawing a white bean, (b) drawing a red bean. Solution: (a) Drawing a white bean is certain, because every bean in the bag is white. (b) Drawing a red bean is impossible, because there…

workedExamples.solution: Solution: The rule is ABC (red, blue, green). After red should come blue, so the gap is blue. Check it: red, blue, green, red, blue, green, red, blue, green – the rule holds all the way, so blue is correct. Worked Example 5: Certain, likely, unlikely or impossible? (b) Drawing a red bean is impossible, because there…

A bag holds only white beans. Choose the right word for each: (a) drawing a white bean, (b) drawing a red bean. Solution: (a) Drawing a white bean is certain, because every bean in the bag is white. (b) Drawing a red bean is impossible, because there…

workedExamples.title: The fair coin
workedExamples.prompt: Amina tosses a fair coin. It lands on heads three times in a row. On the next toss, is heads or tails more likely?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Neither! Heads and tails are still equally likely, because a coin has no memory. Each toss starts fresh with two equal sides, so what happened before does not change the next toss.

workedExamples.title: Order by likelihood
workedExamples.prompt: Put these three events in order from most likely to least likely: (a) it will be hot tomorrow in Kismaayo, (b) you will roll a 3 on a dice, (c) a goat will fly over the house.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: (a) Hot weather in Kismaayo is likely. (b) Rolling a 3 is possible but not very likely (one chance out of six). (c) A flying goat is impossible. So from most to least likely: (a), (b), (c).

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Continue for three more terms: A, B, B, A, B, B, ___, ___, ___.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: Sort these into certain / likely / unlikely / impossible: a) it will rain in the wet season, b) rolling a 6 on a dice, c) a goat will fly, d) night will come after day.
workedExamples.solution: a) likely, b) unlikely (one chance in six), c) impossible, d) certain.

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: True or False: after a coin lands heads three times, tails is more likely next. Explain.
workedExamples.solution: False – a coin has no memory, so heads and tails stay equally likely.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Create an ABBC pattern using four different things from your home and write it for eight terms.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Any correct ABBC pattern, e.g. stone, leaf, leaf, shell, stone, leaf, leaf, shell.

activities.title: Bead or Button Pattern String
activities.materials: beads, buttons, or dry beans in two or three colours, and a string or piece of thread.
steps: Choose a rule, such as AB (red, white) or ABC (red, white, blue).
Thread the beads to follow your rule for at least three repeats.
Say your pattern out loud as you go: ‘red, white, red, white…’
Ask a family member to continue your string with the next three beads.
Keep your string – you can make a new rule each day of the unit.

activities.title: Sound and Movement Patterns
activities.materials: just your hands, feet and voice.
steps: Make a sound pattern like clap, stamp, clap, stamp (an AB pattern).
Try a longer rule such as clap, clap, stamp (an AAB pattern).
Perform your pattern for someone and let them join in once they hear the rule.
Swap: they make a pattern and you find the rule and continue it.

activities.title: The Coin Toss Experiment
activities.materials: one coin, paper and a pencil.
steps: Make two columns on your paper: one for heads, one for tails.
Predict what will happen before you start – write your guess down.
Toss the coin ten times and put a tally mark in the right column each time.
Count each column. How close was your result to your guess? Try it again and compare.
This game shows you that a fair coin gives heads and tails an equal chance, even if ten tosses come out a little uneven.

activities.title: Mystery Bag Investigation
activities.materials: a cloth bag or bowl you cannot see into, and beans, stones or buttons in two colours.
steps: Put in a mix, such as 8 white beans and 2 red beans.
Predict: which colour are you more likely to draw?
Draw one, look, and put it back. Do this ten times, tallying the colours.
Did you draw more of the colour you expected? Talk about why with your family.

activities.title: The Chance Word Sorting Game (Family Game)
activities.materials: your family or friends, and four spots on the floor labelled certain, likely, unlikely and impossible.
steps: One person calls out an event, such as ‘it will get dark tonight’.
Everyone runs to stand on the chance word they think fits best.
Talk about any differences – some events sit between two words!
Take turns being the caller and try to think of tricky events.

activities.title: Design-a-Spinner Challenge
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, colouring pens, and a paper clip for the arrow.
steps: Draw a circle and split it into coloured parts.
Make one colour cover most of the circle and another cover only a thin slice.
Predict which colour the arrow is most likely to land on, and which is unlikely.
Spin many times using a pencil and paper clip, and tally where the arrow stops.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand patterns best? Which helped you understand chance best? What was the trickiest part? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Pattern Rule
rules.text: A pattern has a chunk that repeats in the same order over and over. The repeating chunk is called the rule, for example AB or ABC. If nothing repeats, it is a random muddle, not a pattern.

rules.title: Chance Words Rule
rules.text: Certain = it will definitely happen. Impossible = it can never happen. Likely = it will probably happen. Unlikely = it will probably not happen. Compare chances with: more likely, less likely, equally likely.

rules.title: Fair Coin Rule
rules.text: A coin has two equal sides, so heads and tails are equally likely. A coin has no memory – past tosses do not change the next toss.

terms: Things arranged so a part repeats in order
The repeating chunk, e.g. AB or ABC
The same unit comes again and again
Will definitely happen / can never happen
Will probably / probably will not happen
Two results have the same chance
commonMistakes: Look for a chunk that repeats, or it is a muddle
Guessing from the last item only
Find the full rule first, then continue
Each toss is equally likely heads or tails

realProblems.prompt: For Eid, Zahra makes a bead bracelet with the rule red, white, blue. If she uses 12 beads in total, what colour is the 12th bead?
realProblems.answer: 1) The 12th bead is blue (rule red-white-blue repeats; 12 is a multiple of 3, so it is the last colour, blue).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) The 12th bead is blue (rule red-white-blue repeats; 12 is a multiple of 3, so it is the last colour, blue).

realProblems.prompt: At the Kismaayo market a basket holds 10 mangoes and 1 lemon. Farah reaches in without looking. Is she more likely to grab a mango or the lemon? Which chance word fits grabbing the lemon?
realProblems.answer: More likely a mango; grabbing the lemon is unlikely.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: More likely a mango; grabbing the lemon is unlikely.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan tosses a fair coin 10 times and gets 6 heads and 4 tails. Is that a normal result? What would probably happen if he tossed 100 times?
realProblems.answer: Yes, 6 and 4 is normal for ten tosses; with 100 tosses the heads and tails would probably be much closer to even.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Yes, 6 and 4 is normal for ten tosses; with 100 tosses the heads and tails would probably be much closer to even.

realProblems.prompt: Aisha claps a pattern: clap, clap, click, clap, clap, click. What are the next three sounds, and what is the rule?
realProblems.answer: clap, clap, click; rule is AAB (clap, clap, click).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: clap, clap, click; rule is AAB (clap, clap, click).

realProblems.prompt: Idris says rolling a 7 on a normal dice is unlikely. Is he right, or should he use a different word? Explain.
realProblems.answer: He should say impossible – a normal dice has no 7, so it can never happen.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: He should say impossible – a normal dice has no 7, so it can never happen.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find the missing terms: red, ___, green, red, blue, ___, red, blue, green.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: blue, then green (rule ABC: red, blue, green).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Two bags: Bag 1 has 5 red and 5 white beans; Bag 2 has 9 red and 1 white bean. From which bag are you more likely to draw a red bean? Explain.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Bag 2 – it has more red beans (9 of

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Design a spinner where landing on ‘win’ is unlikely but not impossible. Describe how you would colour it.
Explain the key idea in The Language of Chance.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Now we turn from patterns to probability, which is a big word for a simple idea: how likely something is to happen. Some things are sure, some things can never happen, and many things are somewhere in between. Mathematicians use four special words to describe where an event sits, and you will use them all through this unit. Certain means it will definitely happen – there is no doubt at all. It is certain that the sun will rise tomorrow. It is certain that if a bag holds only red beans, the bean you draw will be…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Comparing How Likely Events Are.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: It is useful not only to describe one event, but to compare two events and say which is more likely. For this we use three phrases: more likely, less likely and equally likely. Comparing chances is often easier than it sounds, because you can look at the objects and count. Think of a bag with 8 red beans and 2 white beans. If you close your eyes and draw one bean, are you more likely to get red or white? There are far more red beans, so drawing red is more likely and drawing white is less likely. The colour with…

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra pattern puzzles, invent new chance games, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why do patterns and probability matter so much? Patterns help us predict what comes next, and predicting is one of the most useful things a mind can do. A farmer who spots the pattern of the seasons knows when to plant. A trader who notices the pattern of busy market days knows when to bring more mangoes. Probability helps us make good choices when we are not sure what will happen. Knowing that rain is likely, Khadija carries a shawl to the masjid. Knowing that a bag of only red beans can never give a blue bean, Omar does not waste a wish on it. Mathematics gives us calm, clear ways to think about both certainty and surprise.

I can as you work, keep some small objects beside you – beads, buttons, stones, bottle caps, or dry beans in two colours. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Make the patterns with your hands, toss a real coin, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every step you take here builds the thinking skills you will use for the rest of your life.
I can recognise a repeating pattern and tell it apart from a random muddle.
I can find the rule (the part that repeats) in a pattern and name it, such as AB or ABC.
I can copy, continue and create repeating patterns with colours, shapes, sounds and movements.
I can fill in the missing part of a pattern by using its rule.
I can tell the difference between a repeating pattern and a growing pattern.
